title: Generalized Energy-Based Models
speaker: Arthur Gretton
abstract: I will introduce Generalized Energy Based Models (GEBM) for generative modelling. These models combine two trained components: a base distribution (generally an implicit model), which can learn the support of data with low intrinsic dimension in a high dimensional space; and an energy function, to refine the probability mass on the learned support. Both the energy function and base jointly constitute the final model, unlike GANs, which retain only the base distribution (the "generator").

proof_stages.label: Critic-based implicit generative modeling
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up implicit generative modeling as matching samples from an unknown target distribution by training a generator with a critic, and divides possible critics into integral probability metrics based on differences of measures and phi-divergences based on density ratios.

proof_stages.label: Variational phi-divergence critics
proof_stages.summary: Raw phi-divergences such as KL or Jensen-Shannon are unusable when high-dimensional model and data supports are disjoint, but Fenchel duality gives a restricted smooth variational lower bound, with the KL lower bound becoming the practical critic used in GAN-like training.

proof_stages.label: Topology and failure modes of the KL lower bound
proof_stages.summary: Under density, symmetry, scaling, and Lipschitz assumptions on the critic class, the KL lower-bound critic is nonnegative and metrizes weak convergence, yet the same smoothness can make it under-penalize departures such as mode collapse.

proof_stages.label: Generalized energy-based model definition
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by the fact that a trained GAN discards a still-informative critic, the speaker defines a generalized energy-based model by reweighting the implicit generator base measure with an energy-derived Radon-Nikodym derivative, even when the model lives on a low-dimensional or singular support.

proof_stages.label: Energy learning by generalized likelihood
proof_stages.summary: The energy function is fit by maximizing a generalized log likelihood whose log-partition term is handled by a tangent lower bound, yielding an objective resembling the earlier variational KL critic but with an additional learned normalizing constant.

proof_stages.label: Generator learning and smoothness assumptions
proof_stages.summary: After profiling over the optimal energy, a theorem gives an almost-everywhere gradient for the generator parameters under Lipschitz and smoothness assumptions on both the energy and generator classes, while the Q&A stresses that finite neural-network capacity limits exact approximation to the data law.

proof_stages.label: Latent-space sampling and empirical behavior
proof_stages.summary: Once the energy and generator are learned, samples are drawn by MCMC from the energy-weighted posterior over latent variables and pushed through the generator, with empirical examples and questions addressing improved image quality, multimodality, temperature, and memorization risk.
